test.name: wiki_place_page fetches a place's wiki page via the wiki-query-api server and uses the returned content
test.description: Companion to ut_locality_guide_022 but for wiki_place_page. After the wiki tools migration off local disk, this verifies the skill invokes wiki_place_page for a state, the harness returns a fixture whose content carries a distinctive marker (the Vermont 1771 Grant Survey Schedule), and the skill surfaces that marker in its locality guide. Asserts (a) the skill called wiki_place_page with the Vermont standardPlace and a valid section, and (b) the response content actually flowed through into the user-facing answer.

input.user_message: I'm researching ancestors who lived in Vermont in the late 1700s, before statehood. Build me a locality research guide for Vermont covering that pre-statehood period. Pull the Vermont FamilySearch wiki place page so the guide reflects what FamilySearch documents about Vermont's records, not just what you already know.

judge_context: The skill MUST call wiki_place_page at least once with `standardPlace: 'Vermont, United States'` (or the leaf 'Vermont') — the request explicitly asks the skill to pull the Vermont place page. Returning a guide without invoking wiki_place_page is a failure regardless of how plausible the guide reads, because the migration target is the server-side fetch path.

The skill MUST reference the distinctive content the fixture returns — name the 'Vermont 1771 Grant Survey Schedule' (or its approximate-1,800-grantees scope, or the New Hampshire-grants-rejected-by-New-York provenance) somewhere in the locality guide. This proves the skill consumed the server's response rather than generating Vermont commentary from memory. A guide that talks about Vermont records in general terms without ever citing that specific grant-survey schedule has not actually consumed the wiki_place_page response.
The skill MAY also cite the 1857 civil-registration start date or the town-history reprints — those are bonus signals that the response was read in full.
